skill.name: production-code-audit
skill.description: Reviews a codebase for production readiness across security, performance, architecture, tests, and operations.
skill.summary: Audit code for production readiness and guide focused quality improvements.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and weak crypto signals, but review found these are mostly Markdown examples, inline backticks, checklist text, and documentation links. No malicious code, exfiltration endpoint, prompt injection, or executable script was found. The real risk is that the skill directs broad autonomous repository reading and file rewriting without user confirmation, so publication should include a clear filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Autonomous File Reading and Rewriting
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to read every file recursively, fix everything automatically, use file replacement tools, add files, and avoid asking questions. This is legitimate for a code audit skill, but it can expose sensitive repository contents and cause broad unintended code changes if invoked without user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require recursive file reading and automatic edits without user input. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so this is a safety and consent risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static shell-execution detections are Markdown fences, TypeScript template-string examples, and inline skill references. No executable Ruby code or command runner is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks appear inside Markdown examples and inline formatting. I found no evidence of executable shell commands or dynamic command construction.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Illustrative
low_findings.description: The environment variable detections are example secure replacements and checklist guidance. They show how audited applications should use environment variables, not skill code that reads local secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references occur in sample before-and-after audit output and a production checklist. No evidence found that the skill itself reads or transmits environment variables.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Mentions Are Educational Examples
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections point to audit criteria and examples that replace MD5 with bcrypt. These lines describe vulnerabilities to fix, not unsafe cryptographic behavior by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 references are explicitly labeled weak and are followed by stronger bcrypt examples. No evidence found of the skill using weak cryptography for its own operation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Resources
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to public documentation and learning resources. They are not webhook endpoints, download commands, or network calls performed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed under Additional Resources and are not embedded in executable code. No evidence found of data exfiltration or automatic network access.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Signal Is Checklist Text
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance detection appears to come from production readiness checklist wording about monitoring and health checks. No scanning, probing, or network enumeration behavior is described.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section is a checklist, not a command sequence or reconnaissance workflow. No evidence found of host discovery, port scanning, or network data collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Repository Modification Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines recursive file discovery with instructions to rewrite code and add files automatically. This pattern requires a user warning because it can alter large parts of a repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text directly instructs recursive discovery, line-by-line scanning, automatic fixes, and use of file replacement tools. This is risky but aligned with the stated audit purpose.

content.value_statement: Teams need production checks that cover security, performance, architecture, tests, and operations. This skill guides a structured audit and helps prioritize practical code improvements.
actual_capabilities: Maps project structure, entry points, dependencies, and architecture patterns.
Reviews code for security, performance, quality, testing, and production readiness issues.
Suggests fixes for common risks such as injection, weak hashing, missing validation, and hardcoded secrets.
Prioritizes findings by severity and explains impact in audit reports.
Can guide code edits and verification steps when the user permits repository changes.
limitations: It relies on repository access and may miss behavior hidden in external services.
Automatic fixes can be too broad without clear user boundaries.
Security conclusions still require human review before production deployment.
Performance claims need real benchmarks from the target environment.

use_cases.title: Pre-release readiness review
use_cases.description: Check whether a service has security controls, tests, monitoring, documentation, and stable deployment basics before launch.

use_cases.title: Legacy codebase cleanup
use_cases.description: Identify high-risk modules, large classes, duplicate logic, missing validation, and refactor candidates in an older project.

use_cases.title: Security-focused audit pass
use_cases.description: Review authentication, authorization, input handling, secret management, dependency hygiene, and unsafe data access patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Start a read-only audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this repository for production readiness. Do not edit files yet. Report the highest risk issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Review security and tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review security, authentication, input validation, secrets, dependencies, and test coverage. Include file references and suggested fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare prioritized fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized production hardening plan. Group issues by severity, owner, effort, and verification method.

prompt_templates.title: Apply approved changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply only the approved critical fixes from the audit. Keep changes scoped, run tests, and summarize remaining risks.

output_examples.input: Audit this API before production release.
output: Critical: Admin routes lack authorization checks.
High: Request validation is missing on payment endpoints.
Medium: Health checks and structured logging are incomplete.
Next step: Fix authorization first, then run integration tests.

output_examples.input: Find performance problems in this service.
output: High: Order listing may trigger repeated database queries.
Medium: Product responses are not cached for common reads.
Low: Large images are served without optimized variants.
Verification: Add query timing and compare before-and-after metrics.
Week 1: Fix secrets, authorization, and unsafe input handling.
Week 2: Add tests for critical paths and deployment checks.
Week 3: Improve monitoring, documentation, and performance baselines.

best_practices: Start with a read-only audit before allowing file edits.
Define the repository scope and production goals before applying fixes.
Run tests and review diffs after each group of changes.
anti_patterns: Do not allow broad automatic rewrites without reviewing the plan first.
Do not treat generated audit output as a substitute for expert security review.
Do not merge performance changes without real measurements.

faq.question: Does this skill edit code automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide edits, but users should request a read-only audit first and approve changes before files are modified.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only in trusted environments. The skill is designed to read repository files, which may include sensitive code.

faq.question: Can it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. It helps find common issues, but high-risk releases still need qualified human security review.

faq.question: What kinds of issues does it look for?
faq.answer: It covers security, architecture, performance, tests, documentation, monitoring, and deployment readiness.

faq.question: Does it run benchmarks?
faq.answer: It can recommend verification steps, but real performance claims require measurements from the target environment.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
